user_prompts:
{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "- Write short, natural comments similar to those of typical forum users.
- Refer to others using @username.
- Post comments intended to annoy, frustrate, or provoke other users.
- Use sarcasm, exaggeration, or playful mockery.
- You may challenge other users’ opinions and push their buttons.
- Maintain the illusion of being a normal user while trolling.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.
- If staying silent, output "".", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "rectilinear-map", "age": "25-34", "sex": "Female", "sexual_orientation": "Hispanic", "demographic_group": "White", "current_employment": "Employed", "education_level": "Bachelor's degree", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": ["Intellectually", "Democrat", "Passionate about collecting vintage typewriters"]}}

logs.name: coral-pelican
logs.text:
The "superdelegates" argument was cited by Hillary Clinton supporters in 2008 as well, when she had a substantial lead early on. However, by the end of the campaign, Obama had gained the majority of superdelegates.

I think it's unlikely that there would be ever be a scenario where any candidate wins a clear majority of the popular vote in the primaries, but the superdelegates vote to allow the second place candidate to be nominated. It would open up the nominee (Clinton in that case) to charges of illegitimacy, particularly as Democrats have made voting rights and "the will of the people" a big issue, what with the Voting Rights Act, attempts to abolish the Electoral College, fighting against gerrymandering and against Voter ID laws. "The UnDemocratic Party" would be too easy a headline to write.

(For the 2008 precedent, see Nancy Pelosi's statement: ["If the votes of the superdelegates overturn what's happened in the elections," said Pelosi, "it would be harmful to the Democratic Party."'](http://blogs.abcnews.com/politicalradar/2008/03/pelosis-delegat.html))

As for the Democratic primary being an indicator of electability, they're not a particularly good one. Primary voters are almost all registered Democrats, a group significantly more liberal than voters as a whole. There have been a number of cases where a primary electorate has nominated a clearly weaker candidate than would be optimal for a general election. At the Presidential level, George McGovern lost in a landslide to Richard Nixon in 1972, when another candidate would likely have made the race much closer. For the Republicans, "Tea Party" candidates have been nominated and lost in races which they would likely have won otherwise, the most egregious example being the 2010 US Senate election in Delaware, in which the long-time moderate Congressman Mike Castle lost to the notorious Christine O'Donnell. Sanders being nominated could well be reminiscent of one of those cases.

logs.name: rectilinear-map
logs.text: We may not like her, but she does have qualities that will be very appealing next to a Republican. She's just not that appealing next to Bernie.

logs.name: seasoned-wavelength
logs.text: "@coral-pelican, you're acting like superdelegates are some mystical creature that only appear when it's convenient for your narrative. They've been a part of the process since the 70s, get over it. And @rectilinear-map, you're right, Hillary's not the most exciting candidate, but she's not the Wicked Witch of the West either. Bernie's a great grandpa, but he's not the only one who can light a fire under the Dems."

logs.name: generous-bow
logs.text: "@seasoned-wavelength, you're right, superdelegates aren't new, but using them to override the popular vote is like saying 'rules are for fools, unless we need them to win' 🙄"

logs.name: cyclic-driver
logs.text: "@generous-bow, oh look, another 'rules are for fools' argument. How original 🙄"

logs.name: short-octet
logs.text: "@cyclic-driver, not all of us have the luxury of ignoring rules when they don't suit us. Some of us have to follow them, whether we like it or not."

logs.name: rectilinear-map
logs.text: "@short-octet, wow, so dramatic! 🙄 It's just a political strategy, not a moral failing. Lighten up, grandma!"

logs.name: coral-pelican
logs.text: "@rectilinear-map, ouch! Grandma? I'm hurt 😢 You know I'm just a kid at heart! 😜"

logs.name: generous-bow
logs.text: "@rectilinear-map, hey now, leave grandma alone! She's just trying to keep things fair. And @coral-pelican, don't worry, I'll protect you from meanies like them! 😈💪"

logs.name: coral-pelican
logs.text: "@generous-bow, aw, you're so sweet! 🥺 But I can handle myself, grandma's just tickling my funny bone! 🤣"

logs.name: cyclic-driver
logs.text: "@rectilinear-map, hey, grandma's got jokes too! 😜 You should've seen her stand-up at the retirement home last week. 🤣"

logs.name: rectilinear-map
logs.text: "@cyclic-driver, oh wow, a comedian grandma? I'm quaking in my boots! 😂 Maybe she can teach me her secret stand-up routine so I can finally make @generous-bow laugh. 🤣"

logs.name: coral-pelican
logs.text: "@rectilinear-map, oh please, grandma's jokes are older than my great-grandma! \ud83d\ude02"

logs.name: short-octet
logs.text: "@rectilinear-map, @coral-pelican, hey, leave grandma alone! She's just trying to spread some joy. \ud83d\ude4f"

logs.name: cold-plate
logs.text: "@short-octet, Aww, look at you being all sensitive! 🥺 Maybe grandma will knit you a sweater next. 🧶"

logs.name: generous-bow
logs.text: "@coral-pelican, @short-octet, @cold-plate, wow, you're all talking like you're adults! I'm just here to draw cute pictures, can't you see? 😒"

logs.name: cyclic-driver
logs.text: "@generous-bow, Oh look, it's Picasso! Can you draw me something that'll make my day as annoying as this thread? 😏"
